cases.title: onboarding migration: The migration problem is caused by partial export scope.

transcript_turns.speaker: customer
transcript_turns.text: I'm not totally sure what's going on, but something broke. customer reports symptoms consistent with more than one migration issue.

transcript_turns.speaker: agent
transcript_turns.text: Okay, I can check a few things with you. When did this first start?

transcript_turns.speaker: customer
transcript_turns.text: the issue began after a recent operational change

transcript_turns.speaker: agent
transcript_turns.text: That points me toward timezone conversion, but I want to keep this tentative. What changed right before the issue started?

transcript_turns.speaker: customer
transcript_turns.text: The confusing part is this: An early symptom seems to support timezone conversion, but later evidence weakens it.

transcript_turns.speaker: agent
transcript_turns.text: Let's test timezone conversion first. If that fits, we should see the same behavior on a known-good example.

transcript_turns.speaker: customer
transcript_turns.text: We checked one example and it seemed to fit, but then another example did not. customer reports symptoms consistent with more than one migration issue, but at least one comparison case behaves differently.

transcript_turns.speaker: agent
transcript_turns.text: That weakens timezone conversion. I am going to abandon that path for now and check partial import.

transcript_turns.speaker: customer
transcript_turns.text: partial import happened to us once before, so that sounds plausible, but the symptoms are not exactly the same.

transcript_turns.speaker: agent
transcript_turns.text: Agreed. partial import is possible, but the evidence is mixed. I need a comparison between a working and blocked case.

transcript_turns.speaker: customer
transcript_turns.text: One working case has the same visible setup. Also, an admin later mentions partial export scope.

transcript_turns.speaker: agent
transcript_turns.text: That reverses the direction. The visible setup may not be the control point. I want to inspect the shared configuration boundary.

transcript_turns.speaker: customer
transcript_turns.text: I just got a note from someone else: a working example differs in one hidden configuration detail. customer has a time-sensitive rollout or business deadline and trust is degrading.

transcript_turns.speaker: agent
transcript_turns.text: That late detail finally makes the actual path coherent. The likely root cause is: The migration problem is caused by partial export scope.

transcript_turns.speaker: customer
transcript_turns.text: So the earlier checks were not wasted, but they were pointing at the wrong control point?

transcript_turns.speaker: agent
transcript_turns.text: Correct. The earlier paths were plausible but abandoned after conflicting evidence. Final outcome: agent identifies partial export scope as the likely operational cause, but certainty depends on the selected resolution type

cases.title: permissions access: Migrated workspace groups did not inherit the Billing Analyst role.

transcript_turns.speaker: customer
transcript_turns.text: I'm not totally sure what's going on, but something broke. multiple users are affected.

transcript_turns.speaker: agent
transcript_turns.text: I need to follow the standard checklist before we discuss escalation. When did this first start?

transcript_turns.speaker: customer
transcript_turns.text: the problem started after a workspace migration

transcript_turns.speaker: agent
transcript_turns.text: That points me toward browser cache, but I want to keep this tentative. What changed right before the issue started?

transcript_turns.speaker: customer
transcript_turns.text: The confusing part is this: An early symptom seems to support browser cache, but later evidence weakens it.

transcript_turns.speaker: agent
transcript_turns.text: Let's test browser cache first. If that fits, we should see the same behavior on a known-good example.

transcript_turns.speaker: customer
transcript_turns.text: We checked one example and it seemed to fit, but then another example did not. multiple users are affected, but admins can still access the workspace.

transcript_turns.speaker: agent
transcript_turns.text: That weakens browser cache. I am going to abandon that path for now and check password reset.

transcript_turns.speaker: customer
transcript_turns.text: password reset happened to us once before, so that sounds plausible, but the symptoms are not exactly the same.

transcript_turns.speaker: agent
transcript_turns.text: Agreed. password reset is possible, but the evidence is mixed. I need a comparison between a working and blocked case.

transcript_turns.speaker: customer
transcript_turns.text: One working case has the same visible setup. Also, all affected users belong to the Billing Analyst group.

transcript_turns.speaker: agent
transcript_turns.text: That reverses the direction. The visible setup may not be the control point. I want to inspect the shared configuration boundary.

transcript_turns.speaker: customer
transcript_turns.text: I just got a note from someone else: all affected users belong to the Billing Analyst group. customer has a finance deadline in two hours and three analysts are blocked.

transcript_turns.speaker: agent
transcript_turns.text: That late detail finally makes the actual path coherent. The likely root cause is: Migrated workspace groups did not inherit the Billing Analyst role.

transcript_turns.speaker: customer
transcript_turns.text: So the earlier checks were not wasted, but they were pointing at the wrong control point?

transcript_turns.speaker: agent
transcript_turns.text: Correct. The earlier paths were plausible but abandoned after conflicting evidence. Final outcome: agent identifies missing group role mapping and restores access
